summary: American photographer (1946–1989) known for stark, stylized black-and-white photographs of BDSM culture, male nudes (especially African-American men), flowers, and celebrities. Close collaborator and friend of Patti Smith — photographed the cover of her album Horses. His posthumous exhibition The Perfect Moment was controversially canceled by the Corcoran Gallery in Washington, D.C., sparking a major debate over public arts funding and obscenity. Died of AIDS in 1989.

works.name: The Perfect Moment (Exhibition, 1988–1990)
works.description: Retrospective exhibition that became the center of a major controversy over public arts funding and obscenity. Planned for Washington D.C.'s Corcoran Gallery, which canceled it under Congressional pressure. It included explicitly homoerotic and BDSM photographs, including Man in a Polyester Suit, Jim and Tom Sausalito, and an image of five-year-old Jesse McBride. The Cincinnati Contemporary Arts Center was subsequently prosecuted for obscenity for showing it. The NEA placed restrictions on grants after the controversy. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Exhibition The Perfect Moment canceled by Corcoran Gallery (Washington D.C.) amid Congressional outcry over obscenity
examples: The National Endowment of Arts withdrew funds for a 1990 exhibition of this photographer's work at the Corcoran Gallery.
His solo exhibit The Perfect Moment that was planned for Washington's Corcoran Gallery of Art spawned a brouhaha over public funding for obscene works of art.

clues.clue: Included homoerotic images: Man in a Polyester Suit, Jim and Tom Sausalito (a hooded man peeing into another man's mouth), and image of five-year-old Jesse McBride
examples: That exhibit includes a suggestive image of the five-year-old Jesse McBride as well as the photo Jim and Tom Sausalito, which depicts a hooded man peeing into another man's mouth.
This photographer included the controversial Man in a Polyester Suit in a series that contains numerous nude pictures of the actor Derrick Cross.

works.name: Black Book / African-American Male Nudes
works.description: Mapplethorpe's Black Book (1986) focuses on nude African-American men. It has been criticized for its exploitative fetishization of Black men. The photograph Derrick Cross shows a Black man with his back to the viewer and his ankles grasped in outstretched hands. Man in a Polyester Suit is another image in the series. Glenn Ligon paired quotes describing their 'degradation of blackness' with photographs from this collection. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Black Book criticized for fetishization of Black men; features nude African-American men including actor Derrick Cross
examples: This photographer's Black Book has been criticized for its exploitative fetishization of black men.
Kobena Mercer criticized a Mapplethorpe collection titled for this color because of Mapplethorpe's 'fantasy of absolute authority' in the images.

clues.clue: Glenn Ligon paired photos from this collection with 78 quotes describing their 'degradation of blackness'
examples: Glenn Ligon paired photographs from a collection by this artist with seventy-eight quotes describing their degradation of blackness.

works.name: Self-Portraits
works.description: Mapplethorpe made several notable self-portraits. One shows him holding a rifle in front of a black five-pointed star (referencing Patty Hearst/SLA). Another taken a year before his death shows him in a black turtleneck grasping a cane topped with an ornamental skull — a memento mori. Another self-portrait shows him inserting a whip into his anus. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Late self-portrait: black turtleneck, grasping a cane topped with a skull, taken a year before his 1989 death
examples: This artist wears a black turtleneck and grasps a cane topped with an ornamental skull in a platinum print self-portrait taken a year before his death.
Near the end of his life, Robert Mapplethorpe took a self-portrait with a staff topped by one of these objects [skulls].

clues.clue: Self-portrait holding a rifle in front of a black five-pointed star (referencing Patty Hearst/SLA symbol)

works.name: Patti Smith Photographs
works.description: Mapplethorpe photographed his long-time close friend and collaborator Patti Smith. He took the cover photograph for her debut album Horses (1975). He also portrayed her nude, sitting on a wooden floor with her knees to her chest, holding a radiator pipe. Smith described their relationship in her 2010 memoir Just Kids. Wikipedia

This artist's many photographs of Patti Smith included the covers of her albums Horses and Waves.

clues.clue: Patti Smith wrote about their relationship in 2010 memoir Just Kids; she supported him financially while he made altars from found objects
examples: Patti Smith described her long-term relationship with this man in her memoir Just Kids.
Patti Smith recounted how she provided for Mapplethorpe while he was busy making altars of found objects in her 2010 memoir, Just Kids.

works.name: Louise Bourgeois Photograph
works.description: Famous photograph showing Louise Bourgeois holding her phallic sculpture Fillette while wearing a long black coat, taken in 1982. Wikipedia

works.name: Lady, Lisa Lyon (1983)
works.description: Book of photographs of bodybuilder Lisa Lyon, depicting her in many contexts: nude, veiled, corseted, holding a giant python. Wikipedia

One of Mapplethorpe's most notable series is Lady, which explores the many appearances of this bodybuilding champion. Mapplethorpe variously depicts her nude, veiled, corseted, and holding a giant python.

comprehensive_summary:
Robert Mapplethorpe (1946–1989) was one of the most controversial and celebrated American photographers of the late twentieth century. Known for his stark, formally composed black-and-white images, he explored BDSM culture, male nudity, flowers, and celebrity portraiture with equal aesthetic rigor. He claimed his approach was sculptural: 'I see things like they were sculptures.'

His long-running friendship and creative partnership with the rock musician Patti Smith was central to his early career. He photographed the cover of her debut album Horses (1975) and she described their bohemian New York life together in her acclaimed 2010 memoir Just Kids — including her period of supporting him financially while he made altars from found objects. He also photographed Smith nude, and shot the covers of several of her later albums.

Mapplethorpe organized his most explicit photographs into the X, Y, and Z Portfolios, dividing 39 works into three groups (X for BDSM, Y for flowers, Z for Black male nudes). His 1986 Black Book, a collection of nude African-American men including actor Derrick Cross, was criticized by scholar Kobena Mercer for its 'fantasy of absolute authority' and exploitative fetishization. Glenn Ligon later paired photographs from the collection with 78 quotes describing their 'degradation of blackness.' His photograph Jim and Tom Sausalito depicts a hooded man urinating into another man's mouth; Man in a Polyester Suit shows a Black man's clothed body with his genitals exposed.

His posthumous exhibition The Perfect Moment became the center of a landmark culture war over public arts funding and obscenity. The Corcoran Gallery in Washington, D.C. canceled the show under Congressional pressure in 1989, the same year Mapplethorpe died of AIDS. The National Endowment for the Arts subsequently placed restrictions on its grants. The controversy sparked a national debate about censorship and government funding of the arts.

Other key works include several self-portraits: one shows him holding a rifle in front of a five-pointed star (referencing Patty Hearst's SLA mug shot); a late self-portrait taken a year before his death depicts him in a black turtleneck grasping a cane topped with an ornamental skull. He also photographed his friend Louise Bourgeois holding her phallic sculpture Fillette in a long black coat — one of the most famous photographs in contemporary art history. He photographed bodybuilder Lisa Lyon for the 1983 book Lady, Lisa Lyon. The most expensive Mapplethorpe photograph ever sold was a platinum print portrait of Andy Warhol. After his death, his estate was donated to the Getty Research Institute in Los Angeles.
